data.title: 3D Systems: Continued Struggles Are Beginning To Look Structural
data.description:
3D Systems (NYSE:DDD) reported terrible results in the first quarter, which appears to have been driven by weak materials sales to dental alignment customers. The company also appears bearish about the impact of tariffs on its business, although this won't show up in its financials until Q2. The poor results aren't necessarily surprising given Materialise's (MTLS) disappointing Q1 software revenue and Align Technology's (ALGN) recent soft growth. It does call into question the health of the business and 3D Systems' path to profitability though.

I previously suggested that there were green shoots, despite ongoing macro weakness. While 3D Systems' share price more than doubled after that, it has now round tripped the move, and the company's problems appear larger than ever. A soft demand environment, mounting competition and customer concentration are all significant issues. As a result, I continue to think that there are better ways to capitalize on additive manufacturing than hardware companies.

Market Conditions

3D Systems has suggested that customer capital spending has come to a halt due to the uncertainty created by tariffs. While recent trade negotiations could help with this, 3D Systems believes that the current situation will persist going forward. On a positive note, current weakness is widespread in the additive manufacturing industry and doesn't appear to the result of company specific issues.

While healthcare, defense, aerospace and AI infrastructure were areas of strength in Q1, demand from dental customers appears to have been weak. In particular, materials sales to dental aligner customers was poor. 3D Systems suggested that this was driven by the decision of a large customer to reduce its inventory levels. 3D Systems landed a 5 year, 250 million USD contract in 2024 related to clear aligner manufacturing, which was presumably with Align Technology. While inventory headwinds are likely to prove temporary, Align Technology's growth has stalled in recent quarters.

3D Systems Business Updates

3D Systems' focus in recent years has been on revamping its polymer and metal product lines. The company now believes that it has a portfolio of industry leading solutions, although is still in the process of rolling some of these out.

Polymer solutions are targeted at the dental industry, a 1 billion USD TAM in the US. This can be broken into a number of opportunities:

  • Alignment
  • Protection
  • Repair
  • Replacement of teeth

While repair has been an area of strength historically, much of 3D Systems' business now appears to be related to aligners. 3D Systems also recently launched its new NextDent 300 jetting system, which is designed for printing monolithic dentures, a 400 million USD opportunity in the US.

3D Systems' metal additive manufacturing solutions are targeted at complex designs and applications where special metal alloys are needed. The company's new DMP 350 triple laser metal printing system recently entered full production. It is capable of producing high quality metal parts with low oxygen contamination and is targeting demanding applications, like aerospace, defense, healthcare and oil and gas. 3D Systems also plans on introducing the larger DMP 500 system soon. 3D Systems will support customers in this area by manufacturing small volumes of parts for certain customers, although doesn't plan on becoming a service bureau.

3D Systems specifically called out adoption of additive manufacturing for AI infrastructure in Q1. This appears to be related to semiconductor equipment and data centers, with metal additive manufacturing offering the ability to produce complex parts that help with heat removal.

Current weakness will likely see a sharp shift in how 3D Systems' business is managed, including resource rationalization and a shift in focus to opportunities with more near-term potential. In addition to a previously announced 50 million USD cost cutting initiative, 3D Systems now plans on further reducing its costs by 20 million USD. Efforts include a consolidation of its footprint, streamlining back office operations and a headcount reduction.

3D Systems also appears to be reconsidering its organ-on-a-chip R&D efforts, which is somewhat surprising given that the FDA wants to move away from animal testing. 3D Systems remains positive about its lung program with United Therapeutics though.

3D Systems also completed the sale of Geomagic to Hexagon in early April, providing it with over 100 million USD in net proceeds. Geomagic had a portfolio of software focused on 3D scanning, which is somewhat peripheral to 3D Systems' core business.

Financial Analysis

3D Systems generated 95 million USD revenue in Q1, down 8% YoY, with services and hardware systems growth offset by weaker materials sales (down ~23%). The large revenue miss was somewhat surprising given that 3D Systems' gave guidance in late March. The company attributed this to a lack of materials shipments at the end of the quarter and greater customer scrutiny of equipment CapEx. In terms of segments, aerospace and defense were areas of strength, while 3D Systems' personalized healthcare and parts manufacturing revenue increased 17% and 18%, respectively.

3D Systems also pulled full year guidance, but given management's tone on the earnings call, I expect revenue to continue to drop from current levels. In particular, equipment sales will likely be weak in coming quarters due to a combination of rising interest rates, banks tightening lending standards and elevated uncertainty.

3D Systems' gross margin declined in Q1, driven by pricing pressure, revenue mix and lower volumes. This represents a continuation of trends that have been present for the better part of 5 years.

3D Systems' non-GAAP operating expenses declined by 5 million USD YoY in the first quarter. The company also plans on cutting costs by at least 70 million USD, split fairly evenly across COGS and OpEx. This could get 3D Systems close to GAAP breakeven, assuming its business stabilizes at current levels. I am sceptical in this regard though. 3D Systems has been suggesting it is on the verge of profitability for some time, and yet its margins continue to deteriorate.

With the sale of Geomagic, 3D Systems now has approximately 250 million USD cash. This should provide the company with a multi-year runway, assuming cost reduction targets are achieved. 3D Systems also has a sizeable debt position that will need to be dealt with in the next few years though.

Conclusion

3D Systems' business continues to deteriorate, and this is before the uncertainty created by tariffs hits its equipment sales. While cost cutting could move the company closer to breakeven, 3D Systems has been suggesting profitability is imminent for sometime, while making little actual progress.

After the most recent price drop, 3D Systems could be considered reasonably priced. I believe the stock could have further to fall though, given the lack of investor interest in the additive manufacturing space. With the Geomagic cash, 3D Systems' enterprise value now sits at around 300 million USD, against something like a 350-400 million USD revenue run-rate going forward. This hardly screams bargain for an unprofitable company with a shrinking revenue base.

data.title: 1 REIT To Sell And 1 New REIT Opportunity To Buy
data.description:
The REIT market (VNQ) has recovered quite a bit since late 2023:

But that's just the average.

The recovery has been very uneven, with some REITs more than doubling in value, while others completely missed out on this rally.

In today's article, we are going to look at an example of that and highlight how it has resulted in a compelling opportunity to recycle capital by selling one REIT to reinvest in another:

Sell IRSA (IRS)

We recently sold our Argentinean REIT, IRSA (IRS), achieving a 147% return on our investment in under a year.

We identified the opportunity back when Milei won the election in Argentina.

Back then, IRSA traded at a steep 70% discount to its net asset value, and we predicted that Milei's business-friendlier policies would attract more foreign investors to the country.

That's exactly what happened, pushing IRSA's share price a lot higher, even as its results were actually quite poor.

Milei's radical measures, also known as "shock therapy," are leading to near-term economic pain, and as a result, IRSA's malls and hotels are suffering declining cash flow.

This is likely just temporary, and we think that its properties will recover in the coming years, but the combination of declining rents and a now much higher valuation makes IRSA far less compelling to us.

When we sold it, it traded at a 30% discount to its NAV, which remains substantial, but there are many REITs that are much safer and trade at similar or greater discounts.

Therefore, we just didn't see the point of holding IRSA anymore. Other REITs remain better risk-to-reward at this time.

Buy Xior Student Housing (XIOR / OTCPK:XIORF)

Xior is one of the leading student housing REITs in Europe, owning a €3.3 billion portfolio. Its biggest markets are the Netherlands and Belgium, but it is also rapidly growing in Denmark, Sweden, Poland, Spain, Portugal, and Germany:

You may recall that we made a lot of money on student housing during the pandemic.

We invested in American Campus Communities (ACC) back when student housing was hated, but two short years later, it was bought out by Blackstone (BX), more than tripling our money since our first purchase:

We think that Xior is presenting a similar opportunity in Europe.

It is not quite as cheap and likely won't triple our money in just two years.

But it is discounted and could be another double within a few years from now, especially if interest rates keep dropping to lower levels in Europe.

The company owns amazing assets.

It has a great management team.

It has a manageable balance sheet.

And yet, it trades at a steep discount to a conservatively estimated net asset value.

Let's discuss each point one by one:

Assets

Let's start with this: the company enjoys a 98% occupancy rate. The company explains in their Q1 investor presentation that there are 6 students competing for each bed in their portfolio.

Such high occupancy rates are rare in commercial real estate. Typically, if vacancy rates are so low, it means that there is undersupply, rents rise rapidly, and this then attracts new developers up until supply and demand come back into balance.

But student housing, especially in Europe, is unusual in that there is persistent undersupply because the best locations near university campuses are typically very urban and already built out.

CBRE recently said the following:

The shortage of student housing will continue to drive occupancy rates and market rents... European student housing is severely undersupplied, with an insufficient development pipeline

Moreover, the established players like Xior have developed superior offerings that new entrants have a hard time competing with.

They don't just rent a room... They provide a real community, and that makes a real difference for students. Their properties are differentiated in that they typically include a lot of shared spaces, including a co-working, gym, movie theater, and event space. Xior then has a whole program called "Baselife" to make sure that the property has a lot of activities for their residents. They call it "community as a service" and that makes all the difference relative to landlords that only rent a room because it is a lot easier to build a property vs. building a property with a real community.

Last year, the company received nearly 500 reviews with a 4.5/5 on average, highlighting the high satisfaction of its residents, and this ultimately leads to higher retention, greater switching cost, near-100% occupancy, and strong rent growth.

Last year, it grew its rents by another 6.5% even as most apartment landlords were dealing with oversupply and struggling to keep rents stable.

This strong growth is expected to continue in 2025 and beyond, simply because there is not enough new competing supply to weaken their strong pricing power.

We love the resilience and rapid long-term growth prospects of the assets. Even a recession likely wouldn't have a major impact on these properties. Their performance is quasi-recession resistant.

Management Team

The management is the biggest shareholder of the company with the CEO and CFO owning about 12% of the company via their holding called Aloxe NV:

Moreover, they clearly emphasize in their investor presentation that their focus is on "per share" growth and they have a great track record of growing on a per share basis ever since they went public:

You will note that they growth stalled in 2024, but that's because the surge in interest rates forced them to sell a few assets to pay off debt. More on that below:

Debt

By US standards, Xior is heavily leveraged, with a Debt-to-EBITDA above 10x.

But just in the case of Vonovia (VNA / OTCPK:VONOY) in Germany, it is important to understand the difference in cash flow stability and growth in the case of Xior vs. other residential REITs in the US, like Camden Property Trust (CPT), AvalonBay Communities (AVB).

When you own properties with highly resilient cash flow and steady, strong growth, you just can afford to use more leverage.

Moreover, it is important to note that the Debt-to-EBITDA in inflated because Xior owns a large landbank and has many development projects underway, which cost it money, but do not generate any cash flow yet.

Its landbank alone is worth an estimated €332 million, representing about 10% of its assets.

And it has €135 million worth of development projects underway:

Therefore, its actual debt-to-EBITDA would be a lot lower if you adjusted for that, and it will come back down in the coming years as these development projects come online.

For this reason, the Loan-to-Value metric is also arguably more useful here, and it is at 48%, which is quite conservative by private equity standards for a resilient asset class like student housing.

Cap rates have now stabilized and therefore, we would expect its leverage to naturally come down further in the coming years as its rents keep growing to higher levels.

Its debt maturities are well staggered with little debt maturing in 2025, and according to current projections, interest rates should drop from 3% down to just 1.5% in the Eurozone by the end of 2025.

Valuation

Despite rising cap rates, the company's net asset value has remained fairly stable thanks to its rent growth and the value creation of its development projects. Right now, it is at right around €40 using a ~4.7% cap rate. (Note that various property expenses need to be deducted from the gross yields below to get an estimate of the cap rate.)

Yet, its share price is just €29 today, meaning that it trades at a 30% discount to NAV.

That's a big discount, similar to that of IRSA, but where it gets interesting is that we think that the cap rates of its properties have the potential to compress lower in the coming years, especially if interest rates drop to lower levels.

Student housing today still offers a ~100 basis point higher yield than multifamily in Europe, but the spread has been compressing in recent years due to growing demand for student housing. High-quality student housing is performing even better than multifamily with faster rent growth in most markets and this is expected to keep attracting more capital:

As a reminder, Blackstone bought out American Campus Communities at an estimated 3.9% cap rate in May 2022. Back then, interest rates were quite a bit lower, and Blackstone had a lot of capital to deploy. Cap rates have expanded since then, and it is easy to see that Blackstone overpaid in hindsight.

But it still shows you that its net asset value estimate is reasonable and could enjoy upside as cap rates compress lower, especially as interest rates return to lower levels. Interest rates are already 100 basis points lower in the Eurozone and are expected to drop down to just around 1.5% by the end of 2025.

As a result, property values have already stabilized and returned to growth in Europe.

It is also interesting to note that for most of its history, Xior actually traded at a substantial premium to its net asset value estimate and this is in large part because the market has long believed that the management's estimates were too conservative in light of the rapid growth of its assets:

If you now expect the NAV to rebound a bit and Xior's share price returns to its typical P/NAV premium, that would more than double your money.

I would also add that the Euro is also historically cheap relative to the US Dollar, offering a double discount for American investors.

Potential Catalysts

There are two main reasons why Xior is discounted.

Firstly, the surge in interest rates.

Secondly, its relatively high leverage.

But both of these reasons are temporary. As we noted earlier, interest rates are expected to drop from 3% down to just 1.5% in the Eurozone by the end of 2025. Analysts expect it to drop to 1% or lower by the end of 2026.

Moreover, the company's leverage has now stabilized. To be fair, Xior was getting a bit overleveraged a year ago with its LTV rising above 55% and understandably, this was concerning the market. But since then, Xior has sold a few assets to pay off debt, rates have been cut, cap rates have stabilized, it has extended maturities, and asset values have returned to growth.

Therefore, the near-term risks have already come down significantly and will likely come down further in 2025/2026 as rents keep growing, and new development projects are completed, reducing the company's leverage.

Upside and Dividend

We think that these catalysts have the potential to increase property values and close the gap to its net asset value, potentially leading to 50-100% upside in the coming years.

While you wait for this upside, you earn a 6% dividend yield, which we expect to be sustainable. Its payout ratio is reasonable at 79%, rents are growing, new development projects will soon come online, and rates should be cut significantly in 2025.

Simply between the yield and the growth, investors should do well over the long run. The upside from repricing will come on top of that.

Closing Note

I am bullish on REITs, but not all of them are equally attractive.

If you want to maximize gains in the recovery, you need to be very selective.

At High Yield Landlord, we invest in just one REIT out of ten on average.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brenntag SE 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Brenntag SE in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: How Our High Yield Portfolio Beats The S&P 500
data.description:
Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

Here's a proposition most industry professionals would scoff at: It is possible to create a high yielding equity portfolio, which over multiple periods beats the S&P 500 (SPY) (SPX).

And if you looked at the Dividend Freedom Tribe's High Yield portfolio since inception on Seeking Alpha in January 2021, whilst conveniently choosing to ignore dividends, you'd be right!

The S&P 500 has delivered capital gains of 59% vs. 51% for our High Yield portfolio since 2021.

However, if you look at capital gains without considering the contribution of dividends to total returns, it would be like counting your salary but forgetting the bonus.

In our portfolio's case, the contribution of dividends has significantly improved our total returns.

The High Yield portfolio's total return of 92% eclipses the S&P 500's 65% return since its inception.

This article will look at performance over time, zero in on our year to date performance, explaining what has driven better performance (sector exposure? stock selection?), and offer some guidance on how to set up a High Yield portfolio that works.

And in anticipation of those that say I'm cherry-picking our portfolio with the best total returns, we have achieved similar results with a low yield-high growth dividend portfolio over the same period.

I'm attempting to write this article, not as a "victory lap" but as an explanation of what to expect when you construct a portfolio which is significantly different than the S&P 500.

So with that being said, let's jump in.

How we build portfolios.

Right off the bat, I want to make one thing clear: You can only outperform the index by as much as you're willing to underperform it.

What does this mean?

If beating the S&P 500 is your core objective (for our portfolio it's a secondary objective after generating high, growing current dividend income), then you need to have a portfolio which looks quite different than the S&P 500.

If you're a closet-index money manager, you'd have a portfolio which might be SPY + your conviction picks on a stock or sector level. So you might have a bit more Nvidia (NVDA) and short out a sector you don't want exposure to.

It's "active" management for chumps who lack sufficient conviction to bet on their picks, or who depend on capital inflows and outflows on a quarter to quarter basis and cannot afford to underperform too bad.

Even if that means never outperforming.

Our approach is quite different.

We pick stocks based on our fundamental belief that they are undervalued and offer stable dividends.

We don't always get either of those right, but we do often enough that we come out on top.

Our approach can best be understood by our motto: "Buy Low, Sell High, Get Paid To Wait".

A clear example of that in action would be our Philip Morris (PM) recommendations throughout the years on Seeking Alpha.

Below is a quick overview of our coverage on Seeking Alpha and for The Dividend Freedom Tribe.

What it basically shows is bullishness for the period 2019 to 2024, followed by a watch/hold period in 2025.

The DFT chart below shows the green and red line overlays as our buy below and sell above targets.

As you can see, we accumulated PM when it was undervalued, and now that we believe it is quite overvalued, we are gradually exiting our position.

Nothing rash. Move in over time. Move out over time.

It works. And that's how we build our portfolios. We find businesses we like at prices we like, which pay us the right amount. We categorize them into two categories: All weather and fair weather. That doesn't mean the stock will go up or down more or less, it is our appreciation of dividend safety.

If we think the dividend is super safe, it's all weather, if we think it's reasonably safe, it's fair weather. All weather positions get a maximum allocation of 5%, fair weather of 2%.

These numbers can be overshot if positions perform very well for a long time.

We don't always get it right. In fact, we have plenty of mistakes, which you'll easily find by going through our history of articles. But good diversification, prudent position sizing, and conservative investing have done well for us so far.

Year by year returns

So, of course, our returns look quite different than those of the S&P 500.

In 2021, the inception year of the portfolio, we beat the index by a few percentage points.

In 2022, we pounded the index. We had strong positive returns, while it didn't.

Then in 2023 and 2024 we had worse returns.

So far in 2025 we've been beating it.

Over time, we've come out significantly on top.

But it's a bumpy ride. And that's the price to pay when you take the road less traveled.

Performance YTD

So far this year, our portfolio has returned a total return of 4.6%, vs. 0.2% for the S&P 500.

It's a theme we've seen time and time again. Our constant rotation out of expensive names and into cheaper ones shelters us from market crashes. At one point, the S&P 500 was at -15% for the year. We had barely dipped below -5% at that point. The February, March, and April returns show this (S&P 500 returns in brackets).

Of course, we didn't have the big bounce back in May, but well, we didn't need it as we didn't fall as hard.

So where is this outperformance coming from?

Sector performance

There are many ways to skin a cat, and about just as many ways we could attribute portfolio performance. We could look at factors, at betas, at this that and the other.

I like to keep it simple and look at two things: Allocation to sectors and allocation to stocks.

Simply put, if you were underweight in tech and overweight in energy in 2022 like we were, you did better than the index. That same set-up made you do worse than the index in 2023. That's what happened to us.

So let's compare the SPY's allocation and ours.

Sector S&P 500 High Yield PF
Technology 32.3% 3.17%
Financials 14.12% 14.9%
Consumer Discretionary 10.5% 3.98%
Healthcare 10.07% 9.18%
Communication Services 9.43% 7.42%
Industrials 7.82% 0%
Consumer Staples 5.98% 11.96%
Energy 3.20% 7.9%
Utilities 2.56% 4.54%
Real Estate 2.23% 29.6%
Materials 1.76% 2.77%

Fundamentally, the difference in our portfolio and the S&P 500 can be understood by:

  • Where the S&P 500 has tech exposure, we have REIT exposure, and vice versa.
  • Where the S&P 500 has consumer discretionary exposure, we have staples exposure, and vice versa.
  • Where the S&P 500 has industrials exposure, we have higher energy and utility exposure.
  • We also have 4.57% fixed income exposure, which the S&P 500 doesn't have.

This makes explaining most of the returns of our portfolio vs. the S&P 500 quite simple.

Year to date, REITs are up 2.41% while Tech is down 2%.

Staples are up 3.08% while Discretionaries are down 5%.

In a nutshell, that explains a lot of why we're up more than the S&P 500.

Stock Selection

But that's not the whole story.

Because our REITs don't line up perfectly with the Real Estate Select Sector ETF (XLRE), and our staples don't line up with the Staples Select Sector ETF (XLP).

A quick look shows that our REIT selection is pretty much on track with that of the sector ETF.

Our utilities did much better than the sector fund (XLU), 19% vs. 5% YTD.

This comes down to the 3 utility stocks in the High Yield portfolio having done so much better:

  • WEC Energy (WEC) up 11%
  • Engie SA (OTCPK:ENGIY) up 25%
  • Clearway Energy (CWEN) up 10%

Our staples positions have also done a lot better than the XLP. Up 25% vs. 3%.

This is down to our British American Tobacco (BTI) and Philip Morris (PM) positions being up 12% and 35% respectively.

Our PepsiCo (PEP) and Conagra Brands (CAG) positions are down bad, but collectively they are about 1/7th of our exposure to BTI and PM.

Remember what I said about prudent position sizing? It matters.

Those are "the big ones", that moved our portfolio. Our tech did better also than the index, thanks to our IBM (IBM) position (which we've been slowly exiting). Our energy has done better (5% vs. -1%), thanks to our Enbridge (ENB) exposure.

Our financials exposure has done worse (1% vs. 5%), our healthcare did 8% worse due to our Pfizer (PFE) exposure, and our discretionary was down 11% vs. the 5%.

As you can see, we're not geniuses. Otherwise, we'd have had a lot more exposure to utilities and our tobacco staples going into this year. We'd moved in that direction, but we didn't over commit.

There are spots where we did worse, and those we did better. I still think our portfolio is well set up for the current environment.

Conclusion: A system that "works"

We've done at best a decent job at being in the right sectors at the right time.

We've done a considerably better job at finding value, and buying it on the cheap. This locks in high dividend yields, bakes in capital gains for the future, and sets us up for long-term success.

Conservative position sizing. Deep dive financial analysis. Disciplined buying at prices which give us sufficient dividends.

This is how a high yield portfolio can outperform the S&P 500 even when it is a "secondary objective" to generating dividends which you can live off in retirement.

"Buy Low, Sell High, Get Paid To Wait".

It sounds so simple, it runs the risk of making us look stupid. But with enough patience, it's worked for us. With less fanfare and violent ups and downs than the index.

And a big pile of dividend income that comes trickling in every quarter, something which matches the needs of people preparing for a retirement where their assets will fund their lifestyle.

It's not for everyone, but it works for us.

Some years we beat the market, some we don't. But give us enough periods, and we believe we can comfortably come out on top, which is, after all, what "seeking alpha" is all about.

I hope you learned a thing or two in this article, happy investing!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PTF: Technology Dashboard For May
data.description:
This monthly article reports industry metrics in the information technology sector, aiming at a top-down analysis based on value, quality and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) and iShares U.S. Technology ETF (IYW), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics. This article will focus on Invesco Dorsey Wright Technology Momentum ETF (NASDAQ:PTF).

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for hardware in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in hardware companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios ((EY, SY, FY)) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios ((ROE, GM)) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value And Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industries (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

Valuation has significantly deteriorated in the semiconductors and electronic equipment industries, while the quality score has improved in the software industry.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum scores based on median returns.

Interpretation

Information technology is the second most overvalued GICS sector based on my latest S&P 500 dashboard. However, the electronic equipment industry is undervalued by 18% relative to 11-year averages. It shows a bad quality score, though. IT services and semiconductors are the most overvalued subsectors.

Focus on PTF

Invesco Dorsey Wright Technology Momentum ETF was launched on 10/12/2006 and tracks the Dorsey Wright Technology Technical Leaders Index. PTF has 35 holdings and a net expense ratio of 0.60%, while the capital-weighted technology ETF XLK charges only 0.08%. The underlying index selects and weighs constituents using a score based on price relative strength, and is rebalanced quarterly. The aggregate weight of the five largest holdings is capped at 25%.

The portfolio is concentrated in software companies, with 45.5% of asset value in this industry. It is also quite concentrated in its top holdings. The top 10 constituents, listed in the next table, represent 43.4% of assets, and the largest position (Apple Inc) weighs 8.1%. Risks related to other companies are moderate.

However, PTF is close to the sector benchmark over the last 12 months. It still shows deeper drawdowns and higher volatility, as plotted on the chart below.

PTF is uncompelling as a long-term investment due to its lower historical return, higher volatility and deeper drawdowns compared to XLK. However, high volatility is a good point for investors seeking a swing-trading instrument in the technology sector.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a semiconductor company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0375 (or price/earnings below 26.67) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago based on data available at this time.

It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 5.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 1.34%; for Investor A shares: 1.59%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 5.04% (Institutional shares) and 4.99% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund delivered differentiated positive returns in a volatile quarter. Sentiment and fundamental quality measures drove gains, with notable performance across Europe and Japan. The highly changeable market backdrop was a headwind to trend-based measures and security selection in the United States. The yield on cash assets provided support.
  • The fund moved to a more defensive stance, which resulted in a reduction in net market exposure and a preference for large-cap and low-risk names. This played out via retained long positions in the information technology and health care sectors against short positions across consumer-facing stocks.
Contributors Detractors
Despite the market turmoil, the fund delivered strong returns. Sentiment-related insights capturing “smart money” positioning drove gains. These insights overcame a sharp reduction in risk in early March as investors rotated out of previous momentum winners. Fundamental quality measures focused on the sustainability of earnings were also top contributors. Stock selection was strong across Europe through a preference for domestic exposure over those reliant on global trade. These gains were supported by positioning in Japanese pharmaceutical companies and banks, which was driven by accelerating inflation and increased rates expectations. U.S. security selection came under pressure, notably around IT names. This coincided with weakness, in March, across quality measures that look for growth across top-line revenues and profitability as well as valuation measures. Notably, IT positioning began to evolve toward artificial intelligence adopters over enablers, which helped to stem losses in March as large-cap technology winners were challenged. Trend-following insights, both macro and analyst and management sentiment, struggled against changing leadership dynamics. A small net long posture was a slight headwind given the market decline.

Further insight

After touching all-time highs in February, global equities declined sharply and ended in negative territory. U.S. market stress drove the sell-off as uncertainty about the new administration’s policy agenda simultaneously stoked recession concerns and market volatility. Combined with cooling sentiment around the AI opportunity, after the release of Chinese low-cost AI models, market leadership rotated as elevated U.S. valuations came under pressure. Unsurprisingly, despite concerns about accelerating inflation resulting from tariffs, worries about growth drove a sharp drop in bond yields. International markets proved resilient as investors repositioned for the end of the U.S. exceptionalism theme. European markets were the main beneficiary, despite sluggish economic data and corporate revenues overly exposed to tariff risks.

★★★★MorningstarOverallTM

Institutional shares rated against 35 Us Fund Equity Market Neutral Funds, as of 3/31/25, based on risk-adjusted total return. Ratings are determined monthly and subject to change. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a fund is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics.‡

Investment approach

The team emphasizes careful, fundamentally-based stock selection from a universe of over 7,000 developed market companies. The fund will employ a dedicated long/short strategy to manage net market exposure and seek to mitigate volatility.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Stock values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. The issuers of unsponsored depositary receipts are not obligated to disclose information that is, in the United States, considered material. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. International investing involves special risks including, but not limited to, political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened for investments in emerging markets. The fund may engage in active and frequent trading, resulting in short-term capital gains or losses that could increase an investor’s tax liability. Investing in mid-cap companies may entail greater risk and higher volatility than investing in large-cap companies, due to factors such as shorter operating histories, less seasoned management or lower trading volumes, among other things. The fund may actively engage in short-selling, which entails special risks. If the fund makes short sales in securities that increase in value, the fund will lose value. Any loss on short positions may or may not be offset by investing short-sale proceeds in other investments. Investing in long/short strategies presents the opportunity for significant losses, including the loss of your total investment. Such strategies have the potential for heightened volatility and, in general, are not suitable for all investors.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 The BofA Merrill Lynch 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index is an unmanaged market index of U.S. Treasury securities maturing in 90 days that assume reinvestment of all income.‡ The Morningstar Rating TM for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-

end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed products monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund was rated against the following numbers of U.S.-domiciled Us Fund Equity Market Neutral funds over the following time periods:35 in the last 3 years, 32 in the last 5 years and 30 in the last 10 years. With respect to these Us Fund Equity Market Neutral funds, the fund received a Morningstar Rating of 5, 4 and 4 stars for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Other classes may have different performance characteristics.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Commentary as of 12/31/24

  • The fund posted returns of -0.35% (Institutional shares) and -0.51% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • Duration positioning (management of interest rate sensitivity) performed well, while credit exposure and yield-curve positioning were flat.
  • The fund maintained a below-benchmark duration position. It had overweight exposures to credit and A and BBB rated securities, and underweight allocations to AAA and AA rated securities. It had overweight positions in the corporate-backed, health care, and housing sectors, and underweight exposures to the taxed-backed, utility, and pre-refunded sectors.
Contributors Detractors
The largest contributor to performance was yield, given overweight allocations to sectors such as prepaid gas bonds and airports. The fund’s duration position was relatively stable and small changes represented tactical opportunities. The overall short duration stance was a benefit as yields rose substantially. Floating rate notes provided increasing income at a time when fixed-rate bonds exhibited price losses. The contribution from credit positioning was muted, with small gains from the corporate-backed sector offsetting losses elsewhere. Overweight holdings in bonds maturing in 5-7 years detracted, given the rise in rates, though this was mostly offset by high cash and cash-like positions. An underweight exposure to the tax-backed sector fared less well on an excess return basis.

Further insight

The quarter could be characterized by the market’s reconciliation of the Federal Reserve’s monetary policy easing with the new U.S. administration’s policies. The process is ongoing, though the market has removed all but one rate cut from 2025 and pushed the yield curve steeper by adding term premium. On the municipal side, while broad elimination of the tax exemption is unlikely, adjustment to some sectors is seen as a possible offset to the budget costs of extending the tax cuts. The market is likely to see elevated issuance ahead of these potential policy changes. With this as a backdrop, we are focusing on bolstering the fund’s income. We have had an increasing focus on low duration or cash-like positions, though as yields reset higher, we will seek to extend and lock in more durable income.

Investment approach

Invests primarily in investment-grade municipal bonds or municipal notes having a maturity of less than 4 years.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Fixed income risks include interest-rate and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in bond values. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the bond issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. There may be less information on the financial condition of municipal issuers than for public corporations. The market for municipal bonds may be less liquid than for taxable bonds. Some investors may be subject to Alternative Minimum Tax (ATM). Capital gains distributions, if any, are taxable.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of December 31, 2024, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

All bonds in the index are exempt from U.S. federal income taxes or subject to the alternative minimum tax.[2] The BBG Limited-Maturity Municipal Bond Index consists of municipal bonds with maturities between 6 months and 4 years.

data.title: 2 Fat Dividends For A Lifetime Of Passive Income
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities.

During the Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B) Annual Shareholders Meeting, numerous questions were raised to the Oracle of Omaha about the company’s massive cash pile. He responded that the cash position is as a result of the profitability of the portfolio of companies and investments.

“We have a great set of operating companies that produce significant cash flows, be it in the insurance companies creating floats or our various non-insurance companies producing significant cash flows on an annual basis. We intend to continue to ensure that’s the strength of Berkshire going forward.” – Warren Buffett.

It is true that a large number of Berkshire’s holdings are wholly-owned companies, but they also hold investments in at least 40 public, and 25 out of these pay dividends. Berkshire collected at least $4.5 billion in dividend income from these companies during FY 2024. The multinational conglomerate’s asset profile allows the predictable generation of massive piles of cash every year, allowing leadership to seize opportunities when they find them. They do not have to depend on financing from any institution to make their moves.

While Mr. Buffett has a tremendous empire to support the company’s cash flow needs, we like to replicate this method of financial independence in our personal portfolio management. At High Dividend Opportunities, we call this approach the “Income Method,” where our portfolio predictably generates streams of income to support our lifestyle; this is cold hard cash that we can use towards a vacation, the purchase of a car, or payment of bills, or to reinvest in lucrative stocks as we see fit.

Let us now look at two well-regarded and time-tested CEFs (Closed-End Funds) that are poised to generate massive cash flows for your everyday needs.

Pick #1: HQH – Yield 14.8%

Despite its criticality in our lives, the healthcare sector in America has faced significant regulatory pressures in the past decades, which have profoundly impacted the industry's operations and profitability. These shifts have always created uncertainty, making it almost a constant factor for stakeholders to navigate. The Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1990 introduced mandatory Medicaid rebates, a move that directly reduced the net prices and consequently the profits for pharmaceutical manufacturers, marking an early commitment to controlling drug costs within government-funded healthcare programs. The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPAA) imposed new privacy and compliance requirements on insurers and healthcare firms, creating administrative burdens that required significant investment and significant adjustments to operational practices. This was followed by the introduction of Medicare Part D, which expanded drug coverage for seniors, opening the door to increased scrutiny on drug pricing and manufacturers' strategies.

Perhaps the most notable in modern times is the Affordable Care Act of 2010, imposing industry fees on both pharmaceutical companies and insurers, mandated coverage for pre-existing conditions which increased risk for insurers, and the introduction of measures to cap their profits. More recently, the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 allows Medicare to negotiate drug prices for a selection of high-cost medications, caps annual drug price increases to the rate of inflation, and implements an out-of-pocket cap for Medicare Part D beneficiaries, shifting more cost burden onto insurers. The common theme is that regulatory headwinds have often threatened the profit margins of pharmaceutical companies.

Fast forward to 2025, and there is a lot of fear about regulatory and policy changes to the healthcare sector under the new administration. There are risks, such as delays in the approval of new drugs and medical devices by the FDA, or cuts to Medicaid, that could create pressure on healthcare plan companies. There is also the lingering uncertainty over tariffs on pharma imports. We won’t pretend to be able to foresee the future, but we can state the facts.

Humans will continue to need care regardless of what the economy is doing. Heart medication or insulin is not like a Netflix subscription that you can simply cancel when the prices go up. Biopharma represents half of the U.S. market cap in healthcare, and a lot of the big-name firms had very strong fourth-quarter results and have projected a very strong outlook for 2025. Speaking of tariffs, large pharma firms have blockbuster drugs that often don’t overlap with each other, and intellectual property and patent protections make it impossible for a domestic competitor to simply step in. The firm would have to set up production facilities in alternative locations to minimize the effects of the tariffs, and this can take years. The pharma industry is capable of passing on price increases to its customers without experiencing demand destruction.

We like the healthcare sector, but are also cognizant about the numerous risks of investing in individual names, which include our limited expertise to understand drug pipelines, clinical trials, addressable markets, and threats to the moat from biosimilars and generics. Hence, we rely on the experts to find the right companies and ensure steady cash flows into our account from the robustness of this critical sector.

With $900 million in managed assets across 137 holdings, abrdn Healthcare Investors (HQH) provides much-needed diversification and regular income for shareholders.

Operating since 1987, HQH currently has ~43% of its portfolio invested in some of the largest biotech and pharma companies globally. All these companies have a large customer base, steady revenues, and a very strong market position in their respective subdomains. Source.

HQH has seen several of the regulatory headwinds and challenges we discussed earlier, and has paid steady distributions since its inception. Over the past two decades, the company has delivered relatively steady payments, with a 14% average yield.

During these two decades, despite violent and unpredictable fluctuations in the stock price and NAV, HQH grew its NAV by 20%. This is because the CEF is actively managed; if there is an underperformer with a weak outlook, the fund can easily swap with a better alternative, and diversification protects its net exposure.

HQH does not utilize leverage in its strategy, and the fund maintains a Distribution Policy of paying quarterly distributions at the rate of 3% of NAV of the previous quarter. This ensures stability during market downturns (albeit with lower distributions to shareholders), providing the ability to recover from them with even bigger distributions.

HQH operates at a 5-year low price level, with a 14.8% annualized yield at a ~8% discount to NAV. This presents a sound income opportunity from an important sector with inelastic demand, amidst market fears and volatility.

Pick #2: BTO – Yield 7.6%

U.S. Banks have outperformed expectations for the first quarter of 2025, driven by several factors, including high trading volumes, strong net interest income due to persistently high interest rates, and robust equity trading revenues. Morgan Stanley saw equity trading revenues increase by 45% YoY, JPMorgan Chase set a record for equities trading revenue, and Goldman Sachs experienced a 27% YoY rise in this segment. All six of the biggest American banks (Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo, Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, and Citigroup) beat Wall Street's consensus expectations on their bottom-line results for Q1 2025.

Even regional banks had a strong quarter, with many exceeding expectations and experiencing revenue growth driven by various factors, including trading and asset management. While some banks faced increased credit losses, overall, net income and earnings per share were on the higher side for the industry. The bank failures of 2023 consumed much of the attention of this sub-sector in 2023 and into much of 2024. Regional banks entered 2025 with expectations to shift into growth mode, especially with the incoming administration promising to cut down on regulation, and support growth and investment. Aside from the noise around the tariffs, the focus of the Federal government on deregulation is still strong, positioning regional banks for increased lending activity. We have already started seeing companies like L.G., Samsung, Hyundai, Honda, Mercedes-Benz, Volkswagen, and Lavazza, plan some U.S. expansion, and this requires capital.

Banks in general have been operating with caution, taking on fewer risks in recent years, amidst the record pace of rate hikes. Looking ahead, we believe this industry is well-positioned to support the economy, but we like to invest with a high degree of diversification.

John Hancock Financial Opportunities Fund (BTO) is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) with 201 holdings, with banks and capital markets being its largest holdings. Source.

The CEF’s top holdings are some of the most prominent regional banks in the U.S., but they only represent ~15% of the invested assets. This provides significant legroom to actively pivot investments into more profitable and better risk-managed companies as the situation with the economic activity and the trade policy evolves.

BTO is not a new kid on the block. The CEF has been around since 1994, delivering steady income and market-beating total returns to shareholders through the DOTCOM bust, the Great Financial Crisis, the Global Pandemic, and the regional bank failures.

A $10,000 investment made ten years ago would have produced almost $7,000 in cash distributions to date. Source.

All this while delivering steady NAV growth, symbolic of the situation where you can have your cake and eat it, too!

John Hancock is one of the best investment managers in the financial services industry, and you can capture the returns from their prudence and expertise through an investment in BTO, available today at a modest ~1% premium to NAV, among the lowest levels in the past five years.

The confusion around the tariffs and possible trade deals will lead to some level of shift in supply chains, with the U.S. still standing to benefit from domestic industrial activity, the extent of which is yet to be seen.

Economies face challenges from time to time. I can’t think of a single stretch of even a few years where some unexpected event didn’t emerge to threaten market valuations, consumer confidence, or investor sentiment. BTO is managed by experts who have seen several such threats to the markets, and have skillfully navigated these while filling the pockets of shareholders with regular income.

Conclusion

Berkshire Hathaway doesn’t pay dividends. It doesn’t buy funds like the ones we’ve discussed either. Yet, it doesn’t take an expert to recognize that Berkshire itself operates like a massive investment fund – generating substantial free cash flow that it retains and redeploys as it sees fit. It buys and sells shares to realize capital gains, collects dividends from its holdings, and puts that cash back to work.

Our investment strategy at HDO is comparable, but our choice of investments are a bit different. We also want our portfolio to generate massive sums of cash flow that we can use at our discretion. The two picks discussed in this article provide solid exposure to healthcare and banking, two highly necessary industries in an aging yet growing economy. And we prioritize getting paid for our time and our investment.

Like Berkshire, we don’t rely on selling off shares to raise usable cash –instead, we build a portfolio that generates powerful, consistent income so we can stay invested and stay paid, no matter the market conditions. This is the power of income investing!

data.title: Rates Spark: Soothing CPI Data And Extrapolated De-Escalation
data.description:
Despite cooler CPI data, trade de-escalation points to higher long-end rates

Longer rates remain subject to upward pressure as trade tensions are de-escalating, while the front end got some reprieve as the US CPI data soothes imminent inflation fears. 10y UST yields have further tested towards the 4.5% upper end of their range, while 10y Bunds came close to 2.7%, which is still some distance from the 2.9% peak of March.

In Europe, markets are currently not even fully pricing two European Central Bank cuts before the end of the year. But at 1.7%, the market implied forward for the rate at the end of 2025 remains slightly dovish, leaning in the context of the 1.5% to 2% range it has spanned this year. The 1.5% was seen just before the de-escalating headline. The 2% market is the peak optimism on the back of German spending plans.

ECB officials try to serve as an anchor amid volatile markets and continue to sound more cautious. Knot and Makhlouf highlight that uncertainty will be negative for growth and will require the full agility of the ECB. Trade negotiations between the US and the multi-nation EU bloc could indeed prove more tricky.

Dutch pension reforms could have clarity soon

As expected, the vote on the change in Dutch pension reform rules was delayed, but the timelines going forward are shorter than expected. Today, parliament will come together to discuss an updated proposal. The vote will be scheduled for next week, likely on 20 May. At least this shortens the uncertainty about potential delays. Approving the proposal would require pension funds to offer participants an opt-out and instead keep accrued pensions under the old defined benefits system. Such a change would mean that a transition date of 1January 2026 for many major funds would no longer be realistic.

The updated proposal keeps the opt-out clause as the default option. Funds can also still choose to go for a vote of approval, which would need a majority and a minimum of 30% participation rate. Compared to earlier versions of the proposal, it now includes more communication requirements. The annexe of the proposal also attempts to address some of the concerns various stakeholders have voiced about the complexity of the proposed changes. Our baseline remains that the proposal fails to find a majority given the broad-based criticism. A lot will hinge on a few smaller parties. If approved, however, we could see some reaction in the long end of the swap curve as market participants reconsider their curve steepeners.

Today’s events and market view

Markets will not have much in terms of data to work with today, but a busy slate of central bank speakers will make up for it. From the ECB we will be hearing from Escriva, Villeroy and Holzmann. The Fed sees speeches from Waller, Jefferson and Daly.

Belgium should be in the primary markets with a new 5y benchmark it mandated on Tuesday. Regular auction supply will be coming from Portugal in 7y and 20y bonds for up to €1.25bn and Germany in two 30y lines for €2.5bn. The UK sells £4.25bn in 10y gilts.
